concepts.label: Missing Value Handling
concepts.definition: Detecting absent data points and addressing them through deletion or imputation before modeling.

concepts.label: Outlier Treatment
concepts.definition: Identifying extreme data points and deciding whether to cap, transform, or remove them so they don't distort training.

concepts.label: Categorical Encoding
concepts.definition: Converting non-numeric category labels into a numeric form that machine learning algorithms can process.

concepts.label: One-Hot vs Label Encoding
concepts.definition: Choosing between one-hot encoding, which creates a binary column per category, and label encoding, which assigns a single ordinal integer, based on whether categories have inherent order.

concepts.label: Feature Scaling
concepts.definition: Rescaling numeric features, for example via standardization or min-max normalization, so they share comparable ranges.

concepts.label: When Scaling Matters
concepts.definition: Recognizing that distance- and gradient-based models like KNN or logistic regression need scaled features while tree-based models generally don't.

concepts.label: Target Leakage
concepts.definition: Recognizing when a feature inadvertently encodes information about the target that won't be available at prediction time, inflating apparent performance.

concepts.label: Class Imbalance Handling
concepts.definition: Using techniques like resampling, class weighting, or synthetic data generation to prevent skewed class distributions from biasing a model toward the majority class.

items.stem: A dataset has a column 'income' with 15% missing values, and missingness is correlated with age (older respondents skip the question more often). What is the most appropriate way to handle this before training a model?
options: Impute values using correlated features and flag missingness with an indicator.
Drop every row in the dataset that has a missing income value.
Fill all missing income values with the column's overall mean value.
Replace every missing income value with zero to keep all rows.
items.explanation: Since missingness depends on age rather than occurring randomly, mean or zero imputation would bias the income distribution; imputing from correlated features plus an indicator flag preserves the missingness signal without discarding rows.

grounding.source: scikit-learn documentation: Imputation of missing values
grounding.note: Describes strategies for imputing missing data and using missing-indicator features.

items.stem: A dataset of house prices includes a few entries with a sale price of $50 million, far above the rest of the market (typical range $100k-$2M), likely due to a data entry error. What is the best treatment before modeling with linear regression?
options: Leave the values unchanged, since removing data points always reduces model accuracy.
Standardize the column with z-score scaling so outliers automatically shrink in effect.
Investigate the extreme values, then correct or remove any that are errors.
Cap the extreme values at a preset percentile without investigating their cause.
items.explanation: The extreme values are likely data-entry errors, so investigating and correcting or removing them addresses the root cause; scaling or percentile capping without investigation only mask the outlier's effect rather than fixing the underlying data error.

grounding.source: Wikipedia: Outlier
grounding.note: Outliers may indicate data errors and should be investigated before deciding whether to correct, remove, or retain them.

items.stem: A dataset has a categorical column 'city' with five unlabeled, non-ordinal categories such as Paris, Tokyo, and Cairo. Which encoding is most appropriate for a linear regression model, avoiding both an arbitrary numeric order and use of the target variable?
options: Label encoding, mapping each city to a single integer value.
One-hot encoding, creating a separate binary column for each city.
Ordinal encoding, ranking the cities by population from low to high.
Target encoding, replacing each city with its average target value.
items.explanation: One-hot encoding assigns independent binary columns with no implied order and does not use the target; label and ordinal encoding impose a numeric sequence a linear model treats as meaningful, while target encoding uses target values and risks leakage.

grounding.source: scikit-learn documentation: sklearn.preprocessing.OneHotEncoder
grounding.note: Recommends one-hot encoding for nominal categorical features to avoid implying a false ordinal relationship.

items.stem: A team predicts whether a loan applicant will default, and one candidate feature is 'days_late_on_first_payment', which is only recorded after the loan is issued and payments begin. What is the main problem with including this feature?
options: It is redundant with other payment-history features already in the model.
It has too many missing values to be useful for training.
It is measured on a different numeric scale than the other features.
It uses information not available at prediction time, which causes leakage.
items.explanation: Because this value is only known after the loan is issued, training on it lets the model see information unavailable at prediction time, inflating validation performance without generalizing to real-world use; the other options describe unrelated data issues.

grounding.source: Wikipedia: Leakage (machine learning)
grounding.note: Defines leakage as using information at train time that would not be available at prediction time.

items.stem: A fraud detection dataset is 99.5% legitimate transactions and 0.5% fraudulent. A model that predicts every transaction as legitimate achieves 99.5% accuracy. What is the most appropriate next step for evaluating this model?
options: Use evaluation metrics such as precision, recall, or F1-score, not just accuracy.
Report the 99.5% accuracy figure as the model's final performance result.
Increase the number of legitimate transactions to make the dataset larger still.
Exclude fraud transactions from training since they are too rare to model.
items.explanation: With 99.5% of transactions legitimate, predicting only the majority class yields high accuracy while catching zero fraud; precision, recall, and F1-score reveal this failure on the minority class that actually matters, unlike accuracy alone.

grounding.source: Wikipedia: Accuracy paradox
grounding.note: Explains how high accuracy can be misleading on imbalanced datasets where the majority class dominates.

items.stem: An IoT platform collects temperature readings every minute from field sensors. About 4% of readings are missing because of brief network dropouts that occur independently of the actual temperature value. Which imputation strategy is most appropriate?
options: Delete every row with a missing reading, since imputation would distort the analysis unnecessarily.
Interpolate from neighboring readings, since the dropouts are unrelated to the true value.
Replace missing readings with a constant value such as zero to preserve row count.
Replace missing readings with the overall average temperature across all sensors and times.
items.explanation: Dropouts here are unrelated to the temperature itself, so time-based interpolation preserves valid data without bias. Deleting rows wastes usable data, zero-filling creates unrealistic readings, and a global average ignores daily temperature cycles at the missing timestamp.

grounding.source: Wikipedia: Missing data
grounding.note: Distinguishes data missing completely at random (MCAR) from data missing not at random (MNAR), noting MCAR data can be safely imputed without introducing systematic bias.

items.stem: A retail dataset includes a 'zip_code' column with over 30,000 unique values, most appearing only a handful of times. One-hot encoding this column would create tens of thousands of sparse columns. What is the most practical alternative?
options: Apply one-hot encoding anyway, accepting the large increase in feature dimensionality.
Drop the zip_code column entirely, since it carries no useful information for the model.
Use frequency or target encoding to give each zip code one numeric value.
Assign each zip code an arbitrary integer label, ignoring the resulting magnitude differences.
items.explanation: High-cardinality categoricals make one-hot encoding impractical and sparse; frequency or target encoding compresses each category into one meaningful number instead. Arbitrary integer labels falsely imply order between categories, and dropping the column discards potentially predictive geographic signal.

grounding.source: scikit-learn documentation: TargetEncoder
grounding.note: Describes target and frequency encoding as scalable alternatives to one-hot encoding for high-cardinality categorical features.

items.stem: A data scientist fits a StandardScaler on the entire dataset before splitting it into training and test sets, then trains a model on the resulting scaled training data. What problem does this workflow introduce?
options: There is no real problem, since scaling is a deterministic transformation unrelated to target labels.
The scaled features lose their original units, making model coefficients harder for humans to interpret.
This introduces multicollinearity among the features that was not present in the unscaled data.
The scaler's statistics are computed using test data, leaking test information into training.
items.explanation: Fitting the scaler on the full dataset lets test-set statistics (mean, variance) influence training data — a subtle leakage even though no labels are involved. Scaling doesn't cause multicollinearity or make units uninterpretable; the scaler must be fit only on training data.

grounding.source: scikit-learn documentation: Common pitfalls and recommended practices
grounding.note: Warns that preprocessing steps like scaling must be fit only on training data to avoid leaking test-set information.

items.stem: A dataset has 'age' (18-90) and 'annual_income' ($20,000-$200,000) as unscaled features. A team must choose a model to train directly on these raw features without any scaling step. Which model choice is appropriate?
options: A random forest classifier, since tree splits threshold each feature independently of scale.
A k-nearest neighbors classifier, since distance metrics automatically adjust for differing feature ranges.
A support vector machine with an RBF kernel, since kernel functions ignore input magnitude.
Logistic regression trained with gradient descent, since the optimizer rescales features internally.
items.explanation: Tree-based models split on one feature threshold at a time, so differing scales don't affect where those splits land. KNN, SVM with an RBF kernel, and gradient-descent-based logistic regression all rely on distances or gradients that unscaled features can distort.

grounding.source: scikit-learn documentation: Importance of Feature Scaling
grounding.note: Shows that tree-based models are unaffected by feature scale, unlike distance-based and gradient-based algorithms.

items.stem: A team predicts whether a customer will cancel their subscription next month. A proposed feature is 'number of support tickets filed in the customer's final week before cancellation.' Why is this feature problematic?
options: It is simply a measure of customer engagement, so it should improve the model's accuracy.
It reflects behavior caused by the cancellation, information not available before it happens.
The feature has too many missing values across customers to be reliably imputed.
It introduces multicollinearity with other customer engagement features already in the model.
items.explanation: The ticket spike occurs because the customer already decided to cancel, so the feature encodes the outcome itself rather than a genuine predictive signal — textbook target leakage. It is not a benign engagement metric, a missing-data issue, or a multicollinearity problem.

grounding.source: Kaufman, Rosset & Perlich, 'Leakage in Data Mining: Formulation, Detection, and Avoidance' (KDD 2011)
grounding.note: Defines target leakage as features only determined by or available after the outcome, which artificially inflates model performance.

items.stem: A hospital readmission model includes 'creatinine_level', missing for 40% of patients because physicians order the test mainly when kidney problems are suspected. Which strategy best preserves the predictive signal carried by the missingness pattern itself?
options: Impute with the column mean and use the resulting value like any other observed measurement.
Add a binary indicator for whether the test was ordered, plus an imputed value.
Drop every row with a missing creatinine_level value before model training.
Impute missing values using the column median stratified by patient age group.
items.explanation: Missingness here is informative (MNAR) — tests are ordered based on clinical suspicion. A missing indicator preserves that signal; mean/median imputation treats missingness as random noise and discards it.

grounding.source: scikit-learn User Guide — Imputation of missing values
grounding.note: Documents MissingIndicator / add_indicator for flagging which values were imputed, preserving informative missingness.

items.stem: A model is trained with one-hot encoded 'shipping_carrier' values limited to UPS, FedEx, and USPS. In production, a new carrier, DHL, appears that the encoder never saw during training. What is the most robust way to handle this at inference time?
options: Retrain and redeploy the entire model as soon as any new carrier value is observed.
Randomly assign the new carrier to one of the existing one-hot columns.
Map unseen carriers to a reserved 'unknown' category defined during encoding.
Remove the shipping_carrier feature from the pipeline the moment an unseen value appears.
items.explanation: Reserving an 'unknown' bucket (e.g., OneHotEncoder's handle_unknown='ignore') lets the pipeline handle new categories gracefully. Retraining on every new value is impractical, and random assignment or dropping the feature loses information.

grounding.source: scikit-learn documentation — sklearn.preprocessing.OneHotEncoder
grounding.note: handle_unknown parameter describes mapping unseen categories instead of raising an error.

items.stem: A neural network uses a 'transaction_amount' feature that is heavily right-skewed, with a few extremely large transactions among mostly small ones. After applying standard (z-score) scaling, what is the most likely effect on the feature's distribution?
options: The scaling reshapes the distribution so it becomes approximately normal, improving convergence.
Standard scaling has no effect on skewed data since it only changes units, not distribution shape.
Training will fail because z-score scaling requires all input values to be non-negative.
Extreme values inflate the mean and standard deviation, compressing most points near zero.
items.explanation: StandardScaler uses mean and standard deviation, both distorted by extreme values, so outliers compress typical values near zero rather than normalizing the shape. A RobustScaler (median/IQR) handles skew better.

grounding.source: scikit-learn documentation — Compare the effect of different scalers on data with outliers
grounding.note: Shows StandardScaler output is skewed by outliers, unlike RobustScaler.

items.stem: A model predicts whether an employee will resign within the next quarter. One candidate feature, 'exit_interview_scheduled', is only set to 1 after an employee has already announced their resignation. Why should this feature be excluded from training?
options: It reflects information unavailable before the outcome, so it leaks the target.
It is a categorical feature and cannot be combined with numeric employee attributes.
It has too few unique values to add meaningful predictive information.
It is missing for most employees, making any imputation strategy unreliable.
items.explanation: This is target leakage: the feature only exists because the outcome already happened, so it wouldn't be available for a currently-employed worker at prediction time, artificially inflating training accuracy.

grounding.source: Kaggle — Data Leakage (Intro to Machine Learning course)
grounding.note: Describes leakage from features recorded only after, or as a consequence of, the target outcome.

items.stem: A rare-disease dataset has 2% positive cases. After applying SMOTE to oversample the minority class, a data scientist evaluates model performance on an SMOTE-augmented test set. Why is this evaluation misleading?
options: SMOTE removes noisy minority-class examples, making the test set trivially easy for most classifiers.
The oversampled test set balances the classes, making accuracy a fair measure of real-world performance.
Synthetic samples do not match the real-world distribution, so metrics overstate true performance.
SMOTE can only generate numeric features, so categorical variables are silently dropped from the test set.
items.explanation: SMOTE should only resample training data. A synthetically balanced test set no longer reflects real-world class frequencies, so accuracy or recall computed on it overstates performance on genuinely imbalanced production data.

grounding.source: imbalanced-learn documentation — SMOTE / Over-sampling
grounding.note: Recommends resampling only training folds, never the evaluation/test set.

items.stem: A churn model includes 'days_since_last_purchase', which is missing only for customers who have never purchased. Which imputation approach is most appropriate?
options: Impute the missing values using the mean of days_since_last_purchase among purchasing customers only.
Add a 'never_purchased' flag and impute the field with an out-of-range sentinel value.
Drop the days_since_last_purchase feature entirely so missing values no longer affect the model.
Impute the missing values using k-nearest neighbors based on other purchase behavior features.
items.explanation: The missingness is informative (MNAR): these customers structurally differ from purchasers. An explicit flag plus a sentinel value preserves that signal, whereas mean or KNN imputation would falsely assign them a plausible purchase timing.

grounding.source: scikit-learn documentation: Imputation of missing values (MissingIndicator)
grounding.note: Recommends flagging imputed values as a separate feature when missingness itself carries predictive information.

items.stem: A ride-sharing dataset shows trip_distance values of exactly 0.0 miles for about 3% of trips, coinciding with a known app bug that logged 0 when GPS failed. What is the most appropriate treatment?
options: Apply the IQR rule to flag and remove all 0.0 values as statistical outliers.
Treat these records as missing data from the known GPS fault, not genuine outliers.
Winsorize the trip_distance column by capping every value below the fifth percentile.
Leave the values unchanged, since 0.0 miles is a valid distance for very short rides.
items.explanation: Because the root cause is a documented logging fault rather than genuinely short trips, these values are disguised missing data. Generic statistical outlier rules like IQR or winsorizing don't address the actual data quality problem.

grounding.source: NIST/SEMATECH e-Handbook of Statistical Methods, Section 1.3.5.17 (Detection of Outliers)
grounding.note: Advises investigating the underlying cause of extreme values before applying a statistical treatment.

items.stem: An analyst confirms that 'education_level' (High School < Bachelor's < Master's < PhD) has an approximately linear effect on salary, and wants to encode it using a single feature rather than multiple dummy columns. Which encoding is appropriate?
options: One-hot encoding, creating a separate binary column for each level.
Ordinal encoding, mapping levels to increasing integers like 1 through 4.
Target encoding, replacing each level with its historical mean salary value.
Binary encoding, converting each level's rank into a compact binary bit representation.
items.explanation: Ordinal encoding directly represents the known rank order with a single numeric feature, matching the assumed linear relationship. One-hot discards order and adds columns; target encoding risks leakage; binary encoding doesn't preserve a linear scale.

grounding.source: scikit-learn documentation: Preprocessing data (OrdinalEncoder vs OneHotEncoder)
grounding.note: Recommends OrdinalEncoder for features with a meaningful order, reserving OneHotEncoder for nominal categories.

items.stem: A data scientist fits a Lasso regression using 'square_footage' (500-5,000) and 'num_bedrooms' (1-5) without scaling either feature. What is the most likely consequence for the fitted model?
options: The L1 penalty shrinks num_bedrooms more heavily than square_footage, regardless of true importance.
The model fails to converge because gradient descent cannot handle differing feature units.
Lasso automatically rescales all input features internally, so differing ranges have no effect.
The model's R-squared will be biased downward due to multicollinearity between the two features.
items.explanation: Small-scale features need larger coefficients to produce a comparable effect on the prediction, so the L1 penalty, which acts uniformly on coefficient magnitude, shrinks them disproportionately regardless of actual importance.

grounding.source: Hastie, Tibshirani & Friedman, The Elements of Statistical Learning, Ch. 3
grounding.note: States that ridge/lasso solutions are not equivariant under input scaling, so predictors should be standardized first.

items.stem: A manufacturing model predicts whether a batch of products will fail quality inspection. A proposed feature, 'rework_hours', logs the time technicians spent fixing defects after inspection results were recorded. Why should this feature be excluded from training?
options: It introduces target leakage, since rework_hours is generated by the outcome being predicted.
It has a nonlinear relationship with the failure outcome that most models cannot capture.
It contains missing values for batches that passed inspection, complicating imputation efforts.
It is measured in different units (hours) than the other engineering features.
items.explanation: rework_hours is generated as a downstream consequence of the failure outcome, so it encodes the label itself and won't be available at prediction time, unlike the missing-value or unit-scale issues in the other options.

grounding.source: Kaggle Learn: Intro to Machine Learning — Data Leakage
grounding.note: Describes leaky predictors as features created after or because of the target outcome.
